utterances: Debbie Cenziper is a reporter for The Miami Herald.
She joined us from the studios at her newspaper.

By the way, if there is going to be another Atlantic tropical storm this year, it will be named Wilma, then the National Hurricane Center will switch to Greek names beginning with the Greek alphabet, alpha, beta, gamma and thus forth; hope we don't get to omega.
By the way, we did invite representatives of the National Weather Service and NOAA to join us this program and they declined.
On Mondays, we read from your e-mails.
During last week's discussion of the brewing Senate showdown over judicial nominations, we received many e-mails disputing the origin of the term `nuclear option.
For the story behind the rhetoric, joining us now is Charles Babington.
He covers Congress for The Washington Post.
Nice to talk to you again.
Good to be here, Neal.
Is it right that Republican Senator Ted Stevens of Alaska first uttered the words `nuclear option' in connection with this filibuster fight?
Actually, we think the term that was used--associated with Ted Stevens was the Hulk, and that's because he has a necktie featuring the Hulk.
He has, you know, the cartoon character...
The Marvel cartoon character; a movie about him a couple years ago.
Exactly.
And Stevens is quite popular--likes to wear this tie a lot.
And the day that he first, as far as we can tell, proposed this idea of eliminating filibusters for judicial nominees, he was wearing that tie.
Sometime later, best we can tell, Senator Trent Lott, Republican of Mississippi, called it the nuclear option.
And that was mainly because it would be so explosive and so dynamite.
Since that time, the thoughts about the origin are traded back and forth and some people think it was really the Democrats who used the term because they were going to blow up the Senate in return.
Mm-hmm.
Now it's like, a lot of people just--`Let's call it something else, the constitutional option or what have you.'
Yeah, it's become one of those politicized terms.
Exactly.
And what do they call it in private?
When they're actually talking with their staffs, what do they call it?
The nuclear option.
(Soundbite of laughter)
The term has stuck, believe me.
Charles Babington, thank you very much.
Thank you, Neal.
Charles Babington, congressional correspondent for The Washington Post, and he joined us by phone from Capitol Hill.
Many of you wrote with recollections of the eruption at Mt.
St. Helens 25 years ago last week.
Kent Snyder was driving to Yakima, Washington, when the ash from the mountain filled the sky.
Because his car lacked a radio--what a dreadful idea--he wasn't sure exactly what was going on.
`It got so dense and dark that I ran out of windshield fluid trying to keep the dust off to see,' he wrote.
`My wife, who grew up in a flat and dusty town in Oregon, said it was probably a dust storm.
We pulled into a gas station, and I asked the attendant how often this happens here.
He replied, ""Oh, about every 700 years or so,"" and then we found out what had happened.'
Dick Bingham was living in Idaho at the time, but could still see the ash cloud as it approached his house.
He wrote: `The next day, I went out on our deck and carefully removed all the ash collected on our picnic table and weighed it on the mail scale at work.
Extrapolating the picnic table surface to one acre of land, we received about 3,900 pounds of ash per acre.'
And Caroline Aarons(ph) was sitting in her high school in Salem, Washington, when she heard the explosion.
Strangely enough, she recalled, 1980 was also the year that Jimmy Buffett's ""Volcano"" song became popular.
(Soundbite of ""Volcano"")
(Singing) Now I don't know, I don't know, I don't know where I'm a-going to go when the volcano blows.
Let me say it now, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know where I'm a-going to go when the volcano blows.
The ground she's a-moving under me, tidal waves out on the sea.
Sulphur smoke up in the sky, pretty soon we learn to fly.
Let me hear you now, I don't know, I don't know, I don't know here I'm a-going to go when the volcano blows.
You can e-mail your comments, questions, margarita recipes or Parrot Heads to us: totn@npr.org.
Please tell us where you're writing from and how to pronounce your name.
In New York City, it seems like you can't walk a block without coming upon a nail salon.
These salons are staffed mainly by new immigrants working long hours.
Last fall, three workers won a breakthrough lawsuit because they weren't getting overtime pay or regular breaks at work.
Another problem emerged during that lawsuit.
Many workers are worried about the chemicals they use every day in the name of beauty.
From member station WNYC in New York, Kathleen Horan reports.
Outside an Upper West Side nail salon, women who would normally be quietly sitting behind a manicure table filing and painting celebrated their victory.
They carried signs with slogans like, You Got Nailed and Pretty Nails Ugly.
These workers are exposed to chemicals far more toxic than they should be...
Susan Kim was the first woman to sue.
She worked at Nail Plaza for 17 years and was fired after she asked her boss for a break, but her troubles didn't stop there.
Long hours in the small, confined salon made her eyes itch and water.
A lot of times problems.
Other workers hands very rashy, you know, skin problems.
Most workers have allergy problems.
There are currently 460,000 licensed nail technicians in the U.S., and the number of workers continues to soar.
Annual revenue is about $6 billion.
Stacey Wan(ph) sees 10 customers or more a day at the Passion Nail Salon in the East Village when it's busy.
She says she's happy she works in a place that doesn't offer acrylic nails.
When she worked at a salon that did, she says the chemicals gave her headaches.
I think the smell - I cannot stand it.
I cannot smell that smell.
Make me (unintelligible) my nose or something.
Milda Nuevez(ph) gets her nails done several times a month.
She's noticed a strong smell at some salons more than others and that sometimes, workers wear face masks as they file and clip.
She wonders if customers should be offered them as well.
Wearing a set of false nails has become a regular part of her personal maintenance as a flight attendant for Jet Blue.
It's like in our manuals, and if it's too short, actually, you know, the supervisor will tell us, listen, you need to like do something with your hands.
The nail industry is largely self-regulated.
Eric Schwartz is co-chair of the safety and standards committee for a nail product trade association.
He's also chief operating officer for OPI, a popular nail product company.
Schwartz says OPI and several other companies recently removed two ingredients, dibutyl phthalate and toluene, from their products, even though, he says, they're safe.
The issues with the cosmetics are not health issues.
They're sort of short-term allergy and respiratory-type issues.
He believes any symptoms workers might be experiencing aren't from the chemical compounds.
And if a salon has appropriate ventilation and uses appropriate work practices, most of the symptoms are easily mitigated.
In Oregon, new nail salons are being required to ensure that indoor air be exhausted outside.
Such standards are difficult to enforce in a densely populated place.
But without more regulations, agencies which are supposed to keep workers safe can't do much.
The EPA's Elias Rodriguez says there's a 21-page document for nail techs on their Web site warning of possible side effects in three languages.
Eyes, skin irritation, headaches, dizziness, even reproductive disorders.
So it's very important that workers take steps affirmatively to protect themselves and minimize risk to overexposure.
So it's all on the worker?
Currently there are no federal guidelines that we can apply under the Clean Air Act to regulate the indoor air in nail salons.
New York City manicurists with chronic symptoms often see Dr. George Friedman, director of the Environmental Medicine Clinic at Bellevue Hospital.
He says some of the ways workers have tried to deal with the symptoms on their own - like wearing those paper masks - don't seem to be working.
The kind of mask that would work is a NIOSH-approved, two-cartridge respirator, which probably if someone were wearing that, no one would go in to get their nails done.
